title: The brief that actually works

spec.title: The brief that actually works
spec.notes_for_generator: Structure a messy brief into a clear one. Immediately applicable to real work. The brief should feel like something they genuinely have on their desk. Full scaffold: give them a template for a structured brief (Objective / Audience / Constraints / Deliverable) and let them fill in their own content.

content:
SUBJECT: AI Gym Day 3: Turn a messy brief into a clear one

Most AI outputs are only as good as the brief you wrote in a rush. Today you fix that.

TODAY'S WORKOUT
───────────────
🗂️ STRUCTURING | 🔵 STEADY STATE | ⏱ 15–20 min

THE BRIEF:
You've got a task sitting on your desk — something you actually need to do, or something you did recently that involved asking AI for help. Maybe it's a report, a presentation, a proposal, a client summary. The kind of thing where you'd normally just fire off a quick prompt and hope for the best. Today you're going to write the brief properly before you prompt — and see what changes.

YOUR REPS:

1. Take a real task you're working on right now (or pick one from the past week). Fill in this template in your own words, then paste it into your chosen LLM as your opening prompt:

> "I need your help with a task. Here's the brief:
> **Objective:** [What are you trying to achieve? One sentence.]
> **Audience:** [Who will read or use this output? What do they care about?]
> **Constraints:** [Word count, tone, format, things to avoid, deadline context — anything that limits the output.]
> **Deliverable:** [Exactly what you want the AI to produce. Be specific about format and length.]
> Please confirm you've understood the brief before producing anything."

That last line is important. Don't skip it.

2. Read the AI's confirmation back carefully. Does it reflect your actual brief — or has it drifted, assumed something, or quietly dropped a constraint? If it's misread anything, correct it now before it produces the output. Then ask it to proceed.

3. When you get the output, compare it against your brief. Does it hit the objective? Does it suit the audience? Did it respect your constraints? If something's off, send one targeted follow-up prompt that names the specific problem — not "can you improve this?" but "the tone is too formal for this audience — rewrite using [X]."

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
Your output is good if:
• The AI's confirmation in Rep 2 accurately reflects all four elements of your brief without inventing or ignoring anything
• The final output would make sense to someone who hadn't seen your original brief — it's coherent, scoped, and on-point
• Any follow-up prompt you wrote in Rep 3 was specific enough that the AI made a targeted change, not a general rewrite

💡 COACHING NOTE:
Structured inputs produce structured outputs. When you dump an unformed request at an AI, it fills the gaps with assumptions — and those assumptions are usually generic. The four-part brief (Objective / Audience / Constraints / Deliverable) forces you to make decisions you'd otherwise leave to chance. That's the real work. The LLM just executes the shape you give it. This structure transfers everywhere: briefing a colleague, commissioning an agency, writing a spec for a contractor. Getting clear on what you want before you ask is a skill, and it compounds.

──────────
Three reps in, you have already built something most people never get to: a repeatable way to brief an LLM properly. If this is starting to feel useful, make a note now of where it could help your career: faster drafting, clearer thinking, better decisions, fewer blank-page moments. That is the work The 90-Day AI Habit continues.

Use only material you are allowed to share with your chosen LLM. Anonymise anything sensitive.
Share the programme: https://harrysharman.com/projects/ai-habit/
Found this useful? You can leave a tip: https://ko-fi.com/theaihabit
review_notes: Clarified that AI Habit focuses on chat-based large language models (LLMs), while using AI as public shorthand.
Kept exercises model-agnostic and usable with any text-based LLM or approved work tool.
Reworked research/reasoning language to avoid over-trusting model confidence or hidden chain-of-thought.
Reframed AI Habit as a grounded free 14-day starter block with a paid continuation for deeper 60-90 day habit-building, rather than a validation experiment.

Positioned the paid continuation as The 90-Day AI Habit: a structured applied-LLM programme covering writing/editing, strategy/decision-making, research/assessment, management/delegation, personal productivity, and practical outputs.
Simplified the 90-day commercial route: join individually, bring a small group for a discounted group subscription, or use the workplace launch pack to get an employer to pay.
Added ethical upgrade nudges to Days 3, 7, and 10: endowed progress, career identity, and momentum protection for The 90-Day AI Habit.
Mechanical repair pass: normalised workout badge line after scheduler verification.
